content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

August 27, 1992

Ann Mills
Issues Director
Commission on Economic Development
State Capitol, Suite 1114
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-411

Dear Ms. Mills:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of the Commission on Economic Development and Lieutenant
Governor Leo McCarthy with respect to the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct that has already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. In
rendering advice, the Commission does not act as the finder of
fact. (In re Ogelsby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

Does the production and distribution of a videotape jointly
developed by the Commission on Economic Development and Vital
Options, Inc., to educate young adults about the early detection
of breast and testicular cancer violate either the mass mailing or
campaign provisions of the Act?

CONCLUSION

The production and distribution of the videotape would not be
prohibited by the Act.

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-411
Page 2


FACTS

Lieutenant Governor Leo McCarthy is also the Chairman of the
Commission for Economic Development (the CED). The CED is
planning to work jointly with Vital Options, Inc., a California
nonprofit organization, on the production and distribution of an
educational videotape. Vital Option was formed to meet the
psychological, emotional, informational, and resource needs of
young adults with cancer between the ages of 17 and 40. The
nonprofit agency sponsors support groups, and offers referral
services and educational services for health care providers
serving younger cancer patients.

The videotape, entitled A Personal Touch; A Guide to Self-
Exam, is designed to educate young adults about self-examination
and early detection of breast and testicular cancer. The CED's
role in the project would be to raise funds from the private
sector to help underwrite the project; distribute the videotape
through public schools, community colleges and universities; and
raise public awareness about the videotape through the media. The
Lieutenant Governor's name and title appear at the end of the
videotape, along with a listing of sponsors. His name would
appear as follows: Lt. Governor Leo McCarthy, Chair, Commission
for Economic Development.

Vital Options will control the day-to-day production of the
videotape. All funds raised for the project will go directly into
the production of the videotape. You stated that the CED will
retain some control over decisions governing expenditures of the
funds raised for the videotape, but that Vital Option will retain
the dominant role in these matters.

ANALYSIS

Mass Mailing

Regulation 18901(a)2 provides that a mailing is prohibited
only if all of the following apply:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
employment or business, or post office box. For
purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
written document.

(2) The item sent either:

_______________
2 The Commission's authority to adopt Regulation 18901 was upheld
in Watson v. Fair Political Practices Com. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d
1059.

Our File No. A-92-411
Page 3


(A) Features an elected officer
affiliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing; or

(B) Includes the name, office,
photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which
produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared
or sent in cooperation, consultation,
coordination, or concert with the elected
officer;

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or

(B) Costs of design, production, and
printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
moneys, and the design, production, or
printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b);

Regulation 18901(a).

While the videotape program will have the name and title of
the Lieutenant Governor on the tape, and is in part paid for at
public expense, it does not appear that more than 200 copies of
the tapes will be mailed or delivered to the office or post
office box of persons. (Regulation 18901(a); Anaya Advice Letter,
No. A-91-215.) You stated in our telephone conversation of
August 19, 1992, that the videotape would be circulated to various
state and local government agencies for presentation to teenagers.
Copies sent to other governmental agencies do not count toward the
200 threshold set forth in the definition of "mass mailing."
(See, Section 82041.5; Regulation 18901(b)(4).)

You also stated that some videotapes would also be
distributed to nonprofit agencies and community groups. However,
you stated that the number of copies actually sent to persons,
other than public agencies would not be more than 200 per month.
Consequently, Section 89001 and Regulation 18901 will not apply to
the distribution of the videotape.3


3 You should also be cautious that recipients of the videotape do
not reproduce the tape to be distributed. Such a distribution of
the videotapes might pull the videotape within the restrictions of
Regulation 18901.

Our File No. A-92-411
Page 4


Campaign Issues

     The Act also requires the disclosure of campaign activities
of candidates including expenditures made and contributions
received.4 (See §§4100, et seq.) Generally, a payment
received by a candidate is a contribution unless it is clear from
surrounding circumstances that it was received for personal
purposes unrelated to his or her candidacy or status as an
officeholder. (Regulation 18215.)

     Thus, for example, where a crime prevention brochure was to
be distributed in an elected officer's district and included the
photograph, name and title of the elected officer, we advised that
payment for the mailer constituted an in-kind contribution to the
elected officer. (Roos Advice Letter No. A-85-057.) Moreover, in
the McCarthy Advice Letter, No. A-90-163, we advised that an
advertisement paid for and circulated by a private party which
featured the Lieutenant Governor could be considered a
contribution where the advertisement was circulated three months
prior to the Lieutenant Governor's election.

     However, you stated that the Lieutenant Governor's term will
not end for two more years. In addition, the Lieutenant Governor
has not filed papers for a future election. Thus, at this time it
appears that the joint expenditure by the nonprofit and the CED
will not be considered a contribution. You should contact us for
further advice if these facts should change.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.5

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


_________________________
4    Moreover, Section 85300 prohibits the use of public funds for
the purpose of seeking elective office. However, there will be no
reference to the Lieutenant Governor's election in the videotape.
Thus, Section 85300 would not apply.

5    Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
